segments.s022-g1: از آن پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'from that time/after that' (آن = 'that/then', پس = 'after'), placed at the start. برآمد is a literary past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic form of از ('from'), and خروش is the noun subject 'cry/uproar' placed after the verb as is common in poetic Persian: literally 'From that time arose from Iran a cry.'
segments.s022-g2: پدید آمد is a two-part verb meaning 'appeared/was manifested' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came' used as auxiliary). از هر سویی is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction/side'. جنگ و جوش is a coordinated noun phrase 'war and surge/commotion' describing what appeared.
segments.s022-g3: سیه گشت is a compound past meaning 'became dark' (گشت = 'became/turned'). رخشنده روز سپید is a sequence of modifiers and noun—رخشنده ('radiant') and سپید ('white/bright') qualify روز ('day'); word order is poetic, placing the describing adjectives after the verb.
segments.s022-g4: گسستند is a plural past 'broke'/'were broken' (here indicating that bonds were severed). پیوند is the noun 'bond/link' as the subject/object of breaking; از marks separation 'from Jamshid'. The clause literally reads 'bonds broke from Jamshid.'
segments.s022-g5: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon him' (marking the person affected). تیره شد is a past construction 'grew dark/turned ominous' (شد = 'became'). فره ایزدی (divine glory) is the affected quality placed after the verb in poetic word order: 'upon him the divine farr grew dark.'
segments.s022-g6: به marks direction/tendency 'toward/into' and governs گرایید, a past verb 'turned/tended' (گرایید = 'he/it turned toward'). کژی and نابخردی are nouns 'crookedness/perversity' and 'folly' functioning as the things to which he turned; the conjunction و links them as parallel complements of گرایید.
segments.s022-g7: پدید آمد is the past compound 'appeared/turned up'. از هر سویی is 'from every direction/quarter'. خسروی is a substantive meaning 'a royal/sovereign' and here serves as the entity that 'appeared'—poetic word order places the verb before the subject.
segments.s022-g8: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'one/someone'. نامجویی is a deverbal/nominal phrase 'seeking fame' (one who seeks renown) functioning as identifier of that 'one'. ز هر پهلوی literally 'from every flank/side' specifies that on every side such a person was present; ز is the poetic 'from'.
segments.s022-g25: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. جمشید را uses را as the topical/experiencer marker with the intransitive phrase بخت شدن (‘fortune befall someone’), so چو جمشید را بخت شد = 'when fortune befell Jamshid'. شد is the past (perfective) form of شدن. کندرو (frankincense) is a noun left without an explicit verb — a common poetic ellipsis in Classical Persian where a circumstantial or accompanying noun is stated without a syntactic verb (i.e., 'frankincense (was brought/burned)').
segments.s022-g26: به تنگ اندر آمد is a locative/verb sequence: به تنگ 'into (the) narrow/strait' + اندر آمد 'entered/came in', literally 'entered into a tight place' (figuratively: came into difficulty or arrived in a confined spot). جهاندار نو is a post‑verbal noun phrase (subject after the verb, typical in poetry) meaning 'the new world‑ruler' or 'new sovereign' (نو modifies جهاندار). The whole phrase thus reads 'into narrowness entered the new sovereign.'
segments.s022-g27: برفت (بر + رفت) is a past-tense verb meaning 'he went away' (the prefix بر- here is part of the old/formal perfective); و connects verbs ('and/then'); بدو is the clitic form of به + او meaning 'to him'; داد is the past tense 'gave'. تخت و کلاه are direct objects in the same phrase ('the throne and the crown'), with object order following the verb.
segments.s022-g28: This is a simple noun list (joined by و = 'and'): بزرگی 'greatness/grandeur' (abstract honor/title), دیهیم here denotes rural holdings or things pertaining to villages (older lexeme referring to country/lands), گنج 'treasure', سپاه 'army/host'. The list continues the direct objects given to the recipient.
segments.s022-g29: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause; صد سالش = 'his hundred years' (صد سال + possessive ش = 'a hundred years of him' = 'a hundred years had passed'); اندر جهان 'in the world'; کس ندید is a negative construction meaning 'no one saw (him)' — ندید is past tense 'did not see' (negation bound to the verb).
segments.s022-g30: بر او نام شاهی is an impersonal/figurative structure: بر = 'upon/on', او = 'him', نام = 'name/title', شاهی = 'kingship' → 'the title of kingship rested on him' (i.e. he kept the royal name); و links the clauses; او ناپدید is a verbless nominal clause meaning 'he was vanished/absent' (ناپدید = 'vanished').
segments.s022-g31: صدم سال = 'the hundredth year' (ordinal sense implied); روزی here functions adverbially as 'one day' or 'once' introducing a specific occasion; به دریای چین = 'at/by the Sea of China' (به + place). Word order places the temporal phrase before the locative.
segments.s022-g32: پدید آمد is a light-verb phrase meaning 'appeared/rose up' (پدید = 'appearing', آمد = past 'came'); آن شاه 'that king' is the subject; ناپاک دین is a descriptive compound where ناپاک 'impure/impious' qualifies دین 'religion', i.e. 'impious' or 'without proper religion' — together meaning 'that impious king' (a pejorative epithet).
segments.s022-g9: This is a coordinated verbal phrase: two perfect participle/finite actions describing preparation for war. 'سپه کرده' (literally 'made an army') means 'he raised/mustered an army' (the direct object marker is omitted for سپه in poetic style). The conjunction 'و' links it to 'جنگ را ساخته', where 'جنگ را' (war + object marker) follows the participle 'ساخته' ('has made/prepared') — typical SOV elements appear reordered for poetic effect.
segments.s022-g10: 'دل از مهر جمشید پرداخته' is a nominal phrase with a participial predicate: 'دل' (heart) + ablative 'از مهر جمشید' ('from the love/affection for Jamshid') and the past participle 'پرداخته' used intransitively to mean 'has withdrawn/been estranged'. In classical/poetic Persian this participle conveys that the heart has turned away; word order puts the affected noun first and the participle at the end.
segments.s022-g11: Adverbial 'یکایک' ('one by one') starts the line, followed by the ablative 'ز ایران' ('from Iran') and the verb 'برآمد' ('issued forth/rose'). The subject 'سپاه' (army/host) comes after the verb — a common poetic inversion where the verb precedes its noun to emphasize motion or emergence.
segments.s022-g12: 'سوی تازیان' is a directional phrase ('toward the Arabs'), followed by the past plural verb 'برگرفتند' ('they took up/set out on') and the noun 'راه' ('road/way'). The verb governs 'راه' as its object; in poetry the object may follow the verb for rhythm ('راه برگرفتند' = 'they took the road').
segments.s022-g13: 'شنودند' is the past tense 3rd-person plural 'they heard'. 'کان‌جا' ('there, in that place') is an adverbial locator. 'یکی مهتر است' is an indefinite predicate — 'یکی' ('a certain one') introduces 'مهتر' (a chief/headman) and 'است' is the copula 'is'. The clause reports a piece of news they heard.
segments.s022-g14: This clause places the predicate 'پر از هول' ('full of terror') before its subject: 'شاه اژدها پیکر' is a compound noun phrase meaning 'the king of/with dragon-form' (اژدها + پیکر = 'dragon-bodied'). 'است' links them: 'is a king dragon-bodied, full of dread.' The order and compounds are typical of epic diction.
segments.s022-g15: 'سواران ایران' ('the horsemen of Iran') is the noun phrase. 'همه' ('all/everyone') modifies them, and 'شاه‌جوی' ('seeking kingship') is an adjectival/participial description attached to the group — i.e., they are all aspirants to the throne. The predicate (their action) follows in the next segment.
segments.s022-g16: 'نهادند' here is the past 3rd-person plural of نهادن ('they set/placed/turned'), used idiomatically with 'روی' to mean 'they turned (their faces)'. 'یک‌سر' is an adverb meaning 'at once/altogether', 'به ضحاک' is the prepositional phrase 'toward Zahhak', and 'روی' ('face') completes the idiom 'روی به ... نهادن' = 'to turn to/face someone'.
segments.s022-g41: از او بیش here functions as a fronted comparative/contrastive phrase meaning 'beyond that' or 'besides him/that'; بیش = 'more' and آمده is implied later. The phrase بر تخت شاهی که بود ('on the royal throne that he occupied') is a relative description: تخت شاهی = 'royal throne' and که بود is a past-tense relative clause 'that was'. Word order is topical (preposing the contrast, then giving the locus).
segments.s022-g42: بر آن = 'on it' (referring to the throne). رنج بردن is a noun/infinitive construction 'to endure suffering' (بردن here = 'to endure'). چه آمدش سود is a rhetorical question 'what benefit came to him' with آمدش (آمد + ضمیرِ -ش 'to him') as the verb and سود = 'benefit' as the noun.
segments.s022-g43: گذشته is a past participle 'passed' / 'gone by'; بر او = 'over him'; سالیان هفتصد = 'seven hundred years' (duration follows the participle). The clause states the temporal span that has elapsed with him as the patient.
segments.s022-g44: پدید آوریده is a compound perfective 'has brought forth / caused to appear' where the implied subject is the elapsed time or events; همه نیک و بد = 'all, good and bad' (همه quantifies the following adjectives/nouns). The verb is transitive; word order places result (همه نیک و بد) after the verb.
segments.s022-g45: چه باید is a rhetorical interrogative 'what need / what use (is it)'; باید is modal 'ought / is necessary' used rhetorically. همه زندگانی دراز = 'an entire/long life' or 'a long life' (همه = whole/all; زندگانی = life; دراز = long).
segments.s022-g46: چو = 'since/when' introducing the reason. گیتی is the subject 'the world', نخواهد is negative-future/present 'will not / does not want', and گشادنت (گشادن + -ت) is the infinitive construction 'to open / to reveal' with راز as its object ('your secret' / 'the secret'); literally 'since the world will not wish to open (reveal) your secret.'
segments.s022-g47: همی is a continuous/progressive adverb 'continually / always'. پروراندت is the past/transitive verb 'nurtured / raised you' (with the enclitic -ت = 'you'). با شهد و نوش = 'with honey and drink' (با instrumental 'with').
segments.s022-g48: جز = 'except / nothing but'. آواز نرمت = 'a soft/gentle voice or song' (نرمت = 'soft/gentle'). نیاید = 'does not come' (negative present), and به گوش = 'to the ear' i.e. 'reaches the ear'. The clause says only gentle sounds reach/listen to you.
segments.s022-g33: نهان گشته بود is a compound perfect/passive: نهان 'hidden' + گشته (perfect participle/become) + بود (past of 'to be') → 'had been hidden' or 'was hidden'; از بد اژدها uses از to mark cause ('because of' or 'by' in poetic usage) with بد اژدها = 'the dragon's evil' (genitive relation implied).
segments.s022-g34: نیامد is a negative past 'did not come' (here 'did not occur/arrive'); به فرجام = 'to an end' / 'in the end'; هم adds the sense 'also/even' or stress ('nor even'); ز او = 'from him/it' (poetic preposition z = from); رها here is a nominal sense 'release/freedom' or participial 'freed' depending on reading — together: 'in the end release did not come from it to him' → 'he was not freed from it.'
segments.s022-g17: This is a verbal clause in past tense with a prepositional phrase marking purpose/office: 'به شاهی' ('to/for kingship') uses به + abstract noun to indicate the office he is being acclaimed into. 'بر او' places the target ('upon him') before the verb, a common poetic word order. 'آفرین خواندند' is a past plural verb phrase ('they uttered acclamations' / 'they cried “bravo”'); خواندند is 3rd-person plural past of خواندن used here as 'utter/say/perform' acclamation.
segments.s022-g18: Another past plural clause with object-before-verb order typical of epic poetry: 'ورا' (to him) + 'شاه ایران‌زمین' (the title 'King of the Iran-land') is the name they give him, and 'خواندند' again is the past plural 'they called' — here specifically 'they named/called him [such]'.
segments.s022-g19: Classical/poetic temporal clause: 'کی' is used as 'when' here. 'اژدهافش' is a compound epithet (اژدها + فش) meaning 'dragon-breathing' or 'dragon-like' and functions as the subject; 'بیامد' is the past tense 'came'. 'چو باد' ('like/as the wind') is a simile modifying the manner of coming.
segments.s022-g20: Standard transitive past clause: the preposition phrase 'به ایران‌زمین' ('to/onto the land of Iran') names the domain, 'تاج' is the direct object 'crown', 'بر سر' ('on [the] head') gives the placement, and 'نهاد' is the past of نهادن 'to place/put' (here ‘he placed/put on’).
segments.s022-g21: A noun phrase formed by two coordinated sources: 'از ایران و از تازیان' ('from Iran and from the Tazi/Arabs') followed by 'لشکری' ('an army/troops'). The repeated از emphasizes separate contingents; adjective-like لشکری specifies the kind of force.
segments.s022-g22: Verb phrase in past tense: 'گزین کرد' is a compound meaning 'he chose/selected' (گزین + کرد). 'گرد' here is an adverbial/poetic particle meaning 'around/about' or 'gathering about' (i.e., he assembled from everywhere). 'از همه کشوری' ('from every country/realm') specifies the breadth of recruitment; word order places the adverbial 'گرد' before the source phrase for emphasis.
segments.s022-g23: 'سوی' is the preposition 'toward'; 'تخت جمشید' is the compound noun 'Jamshid's throne'. 'بنهاد' is the past form of نهادن ('he set/placed' or here 'he turned/laid [his] face]') and combines with 'روی' ('face') — 'بنهاد روی' = 'he turned/set his face (toward)'. The verb precedes its (poetic) object in this order.
segments.s022-g24: Comparative clause introduced by 'چو' ('as/like'): 'چو انگشتری' = 'as/like a ring' (انگشتری used adjectivally 'ring-like'). 'کرد' is the past 'made/did' and 'گیتی' is the object 'the world'. The final token 'بروی' in the manuscript line functions as 'بر وی' ('upon him' / 'on it') in sense, but the provided sense list does not match that use (it appears to be a fossilized orthography); here it should be read as the locative 'on/upon'.
segments.s022-g51: بدو here is the short form of به + او meaning “to him” (a dative/goal clitic). شاد باشی is 2nd-person singular present-subjunctive/optative (used as a wish or gentle command) literally “may you be glad” or “be glad.” و links the clauses. نازی is a noun/adjective “coquettishness” or “playful coyness”; the following بدوی is the same dative idea (به + او with an enclitic yod in poetry) placing the coyness toward him. The order (dative before/after the phrase) and the repeated short-form dative are typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s022-g52: همان is the demonstrative “that same.” راز دل is a genitive phrase “secret of (the) heart” with راز as head and دل as possessor; را is the definite/object marker attached to the object. گشایی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive/optative (here functioning as an instruction/urge) meaning “you open/reveal.” The final بدوی again functions as the dative “to him” — the verb گشایی governs راز دل را and the dative indicates the recipient.
segments.s022-g53: یکی is an indefinite pronoun “a certain” or “someone.” نغز بازی is an adjective + noun phrase, “a dainty/clever play/trick.” برون is an adverb/preverb “out/forth” placed before the verb, and آورد is the 3rd-person singular past tense “(he/it) brought” or “caused to bring forth.” The subject is omitted (typical in Persian verse) and is understood from context.
segments.s022-g54: به دلت is به + دل + -ت (the 2nd-person possessive suffix) = “into your heart” (به here has the sense ‘towards/into’). اندرون reinforces interiority (“within/inside”). درد و خون are the coordinated objects “pain and blood”; آورد is again 3rd-person past “brought” (the same verb as the previous segment, with the trick as its cause). The construction means the trick caused pain and bleeding in your heart — خون is figurative here.
segments.s022-g55: دلم is دل + -م, “my heart” (possessive suffix). سیر شد is a two-word predicate: سیر (sated/full) + شد (became) → “became sated” / “has had enough.” زین is the archaic/poetic contraction of از + این (“from this”); سرای is “house/dwelling/abode” and سپنج is an epithet/name following it (an archaic or poetic word here). The whole phrase says the speaker’s heart has become full/sated from this particular dwelling (سرای سپنج).
segments.s022-g56: خدایا is the vocative “O God.” مرا is the object “me” (م + را, here split in the tokenization), with را marking the object. زود means “soon/quickly.” برهان in this context is a verbal plea “deliver/rescue (me)” (note: the provided sense list gave a nominal meaning, but in the poem it functions as an imperative/optative verb). ز is the poetic form of از “from,” and رنج is “suffering/torment.” The clause is a direct prayer: “O God, quickly deliver me from suffering.”
segments.s022-g35: چو here is the conjunction 'when'. ضحاکش is ضحاک + the enclitic -ش (him), i.e. 'Zahhak (seized) him' or more literally 'his Zahhak' used as agent; آورد (past of آوردن) combines with به چنگ to form the idiom 'به چنگ آورد' = 'seize, capture'. ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly'. The preposition به + چنگ expresses the idea 'into [his] grasp' (instrumental/locative sense).
segments.s022-g36: یکایک is an emphatic adverb 'one by one' / 'not even one' used here to intensify the negation. ندادش is the past negative 'did not give' with the enclitic object -ش ('him'). زمانی functions as 'a moment' / 'any time', and درنگ is the noun 'pause, delay' — together 'زمانی درنگ' = 'a moment's pause'. The whole phrase says he did not grant him even a moment's respite.
segments.s022-g37: به here introduces the instrument 'with/by' (بِـ...), and اره‌ش is اره (saw) + -ش 'his/its saw' (possessive enclitic). سراسر is an adverb 'throughout, entirely'. به دو نیم کرد is a causative phrase: به + دو نیم ('into two halves') and کرد (past 'made/caused') — 'caused him to be split into two halves' (i.e. cut him cleanly in two).
segments.s022-g38: جهان را is the direct object 'the world' marked with را. از او پاک means 'clean/purged from him' (از + او = 'of/from him'), where پاک functions adverbially as 'completely/cleansed'. بی‌بیم is a compound adjective 'without fear, fearless'. کرد is the past verb 'made/caused' — together: 'made the world rid of him and entirely free of fear.'
segments.s022-g39: شد is the past of شدن 'became' or 'came to pass'. آن تخت شاهی is a noun phrase 'that throne of kingship' (آن = 'that/the'), and the conjunction و links it to آن دستگاه 'that court/establishment' — both are the things that 'came to be' or 'stood there'.
segments.s022-g40: زمانه is personified as 'time' or 'fortune' acting as the agent. ربودش is ربود + -ش = 'carried/stole it away'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and بیجاده کاه is an image 'roadless straw' (literally 'straw without a road') — a poetic simile meaning easily blown away or worthless like chaff; thus 'time carried it off like chaff.'
segments.s022-g49: یکایک is 'one by one' / 'each and every', functioning adverbially. چو گوئی که is a fixed poetic sequence 'as if (one) were to say that' (چو = 'as', گوئی = 'as if/it seems', که = 'that'). گسترد is an archaic past form 'spread' (from گستراندن), and مهر here means 'love/kindness/favour' — 'as if he had spread kindness.'
segments.s022-g50: نخواهد نمودن is the negative auxiliary (نخواهد) plus infinitive (نمودن) giving a modal/future-in-past sense 'would not show' or 'did not wish to show'; this is a poetic ordering (auxiliary before infinitive). به بد نیز چهر literally 'to the bad also (his) face' — 'not to show an unpleasant/unfriendly face even to the wicked' — where به marks the indirect/recipient phrase and چهر = 'face/appearance'.

sentences.s022-s1: From that time a cry arose from Iran; from every quarter war and tumult appeared.
sentences.s022-s2: The radiant white day turned dark; the bonds with Jamshid were broken.
sentences.s022-s3: The divine glory darkened upon him; he turned toward crookedness and folly.
sentences.s022-s4: From every quarter a sovereign appeared; from every flank there came one seeking fame.
sentences.s022-s13: When fortune befell Jamshid, frankincense was present; into a narrow place came a new world‑ruler.
sentences.s022-s14: He went away and gave him the throne and the crown, greatness and rural domains, and treasure and an army.
sentences.s022-s15: When a hundred years had passed, no one in the world saw him; he bore the name of kingship, yet he himself was vanished.
sentences.s022-s16: In the hundredth year, one day, by the Sea of China that impious king appeared.
sentences.s022-s5: He mustered an army and made ready for battle; his heart had turned away from love of Jamshid.
sentences.s022-s6: One by one, from Iran the hosts set forth; they took the road toward the Arabs.
sentences.s022-s7: They heard that there was a certain chief; the king, dragon-bodied, was full of terror.
sentences.s022-s8: All Iran's horsemen, all desirous of kingship, at once turned toward Zahhak.
sentences.s022-s21: What further benefit did he gain from enduring hardship on that royal throne?
sentences.s022-s22: Seven hundred years had passed over him, bringing forth all things, good and bad.
sentences.s022-s23: What use is a long life, when the world will not reveal your secret?
sentences.s022-s24: It has continually nourished you with honey and sweet drink; nothing but a gentle voice reaches the ear.
sentences.s022-s17: He had been hidden by the dragon's evil; in the end he was not freed from it.
sentences.s022-s9: They hailed him for kingship with acclamations, and they called him King of the Iranian realm.
sentences.s022-s10: When his dragon-breath came like the wind, he placed the crown on the head of the land of Iran.
sentences.s022-s11: From Iran and from the Arabs he picked an army; he gathered (troops) from every land.
sentences.s022-s12: He turned his face toward Jamshid’s throne; when he made the world like a ring upon (him).
sentences.s022-s26: Be joyful toward him and play coy toward him; disclose to him that same secret of the heart.
sentences.s022-s27: A certain clever trick came out, and into your heart it brought pain and blood.
sentences.s022-s28: My heart is sated with this dwelling of Sepanj; O God, quickly deliver me from suffering.
sentences.s022-s18: When Zahhak seized him suddenly, he gave him no time even for a moment's pause.
sentences.s022-s19: With his saw he cleft him cleanly in two and cleansed the world of him, making it wholly free and without fear.
sentences.s022-s20: That throne of kingship and that court came into being, and the ages carried it off like chaff on a roadless path.
sentences.s022-s25: One by one, as if saying he had spread goodwill, he would not show an unfriendly face even to the wicked.
